column2: Marina had previously dated an artist who painted vague pictures with unknown substances and exhibited, if not in the Louvre, then near the Louvre, in one of those galleries that hide somewhere in the little streets by the royal palace.
The artist was a hopeless socialist and a womanizer, sought women all over the world and suffered from chronic depression.
When he got drunk, he blamed his mother, who, by his account, had never really wanted him.
Money, however, despite his rejection of capitalism, the artist did not disdain, because without money the women weren't all that eager to give him their love.
When his money ran out, Marina met Aiven at an art conference.
He was considered a young, promising photo-artist and was at that time photographing prostitutes.

Marina, stewing in the artistic cauldron, also began doing some performances, did two stretches of fifteen days each in Russia, until she requested political asylum in France, which gladly opened its gates to creative people, afraid of missing the next rising star.
— I am against dictatorship, — Marina said, pulling her top up over her bare shoulder, when everyone had already sat down at the table.
— The world is militarizing.
Aiven looked at his beloved the way an owner looks at a little dog taking part in a show.
— Why did he come?
— the grandmother asked Lilichka, when she came into the kitchen for glasses to offer the guests a drink.
— Granny, your whisper can be heard all the way to Mrs. Darochka's, — Lilichka hissed.
— So what, — Miyechka whispered from the side, — he and Lilichka lived together for a year, he stopped by to visit.
— Big deal, — the grandmother whispered dramatically.
— As if it's anything, who hasn't slept with whom for a year?
Aiven wanted to show off his liberal character by hugging Lilichka, who, though flushed and disheveled, was still as beautiful as before.
Lilichka also wanted to think that she had a liberal character, and she fought off the irresistible desire for Marina and Aiven to clear off as quickly as possible.
Meanwhile, while Lilichka struggled with her illiberal desire, and Miyechka with her disgust for Marina, a forced conversation about art was carried on.
Aiven told about the hard lot of whores.
And about which respectable organizations financed his work.
Marina brought an A2-format folder from the car.
In the folder were her drawings.
Miyechka, forcing herself, took an interest in the drawings.
Marko ran up to Marina.
Lilichka glanced detachedly at the children — they were all dirty, tear-stained and somewhat feral.
Even Roberto, that perfectly behaved, calm child, now looked like a little savage.
On one sheet there was a large red circle.
— Even!
— barked Marko, glancing at the sheet with his prickly eyes.
— Good gir-rl!
— he was clearly mimicking the intonations of the drawing teacher.
Lilichka turned away so as not to laugh.
— This is a window into blood, — said Marina.
It was for the 'window into blood,' which she'd painted on the roadway, that she'd done her first fifteen days in her historical homeland.
— The politicians don't want to hear the truth, — said Marina.
Miyechka couldn't think of how to respond, and caught herself thinking that she physically couldn't make herself open her mouth.
Lilichka was asking something about their mutual acquaintances.

It turned out she knew the artist Marina had dated before Aiven, but they no longer saw each other, because he'd gone to Mexico, where he'd found some other woman artist who sculpts pots with her breasts.
— Pots — with her breasts?
— here even Miyechka perked up.

It turned out that the artist, having sizeable breasts, lays them into fresh clay, and then, when the clay sets a little, pulls both breasts out of the holes, which they yield to easily thanks to their soft structure.
— I could do that too, — said Miyechka.
— Sculpt little tea bowls.
— That's why you have no grand artistic future, — said Lilichka.
During the conversation about breasts, Nati appeared on the horizon, and behind her — Greg with the naked woman.
He obediently followed his mother around the yard and set the woman down wherever his mom told him to.
Nati would look at the woman for a moment, and then again tell Greg where to carry her.
So they fussed about with her until Nati came up to the table and kissed both guests on both cheeks.
Then she asked Marina:
— Bonita?
— pointing at the plaster woman.
Marina rounded her eyes at such kitsch and looked condescendingly at the breasts.
— Nati brought this from a Roman museum, they made a capital investment, — said Miyechka, glancing at her sister.
The latter held back a smile and nodded.
Marina closed the folder with the 'window into blood,' deflated.
While they were still sitting, Marta came out, paying no attention whatsoever to the guests, and began walking around the house.
Marta looked like a ghost — pale and very large.
— This is my cousin, — Lilichka explained to Aiven.
The children began running after Marta.
When Fumitaka joined them, the spectacle became completely surreal.
— And whose are the children?
— asked Aiven.
Of the children he recognized only Marko.
— All sorts of children, — Lilichka retorted quickly, — mostly mine.
Marko recognized Aiven and managed to present him with an earthworm.
The worm writhed on Aiven's palm, driving Marina to distraction.
Fumitaka, having once more come out after Marta to the side of the house that faced the table with the guests, bowed to everyone and obediently followed Marta.
Marta just kept walking, even when Marina and Aiven got into the car.
And only then did Fumitaka run up to Lilichka and say that Marta probably didn't want to, but he thought the children would be born soon.
Lilichka flew into the house and grabbed the key to the red Honda.
— What is it?
— the grandmother cried out.
— Marta!
— What, already?
— Already!
— Then let him drive her!
Fumitaka didn't know how to drive.
The Apostles
In moments of distress the grandmother liked to remember how all her children, grandchildren and great-grandchildren had been carried home from the maternity hospital.
— You, my dear, — she said to Miyechka, — frightened me.
I look — and they're carrying enormous cheeks!
And then I saw that behind those cheeks there were little eyes too.
And a little nose.
And you, Lilichka, I took you all bundled up and thought: is there anyone in there, or have they just slipped me an empty blanket?
The grandmother began telling about everyone in turn: about cheeks, legs, little fingers, eyes, fuzz, bellies, smacking, sucking, dimples on chins, little nails.
Only talk about newborns could distract her from her worries.
Marta's children were born seven minutes apart.
First a chubby-cheeked, fat boy was born, and after him — another, exactly the same chubby-cheeked, fat boy.
Fumitaka nearly fainted and lay for a long time in the nurses' room, unable to get up.
Marta looked at the children haughtily and with surprise.
That's how cats giving birth for the first time look at their kittens.
Miyechka meanwhile was thinking about that imaginary girl she used to lead around the streets.
And about Danylo.
About how he came home, how neatly he hung up his jacket, how he folded his socks after the wash, how he always fell asleep in one position and never snored.
She thought about how calm she ought to feel with him.
So why did it regularly seem to her that something was about to change drastically, that she was standing on the edge of an abyss and would now slip down into it, leaving everything behind.

But the fog would clear, and in the place of the abyss there turned out to be only a gentle little gully, into which you could simply descend and, without much effort, climb back out onto the plateau.
She wanted a child for the time when a path without weeds and nettles would be visible all the way to the horizon.
Miyechka understood that such a thing would never be.
She thought about the women who lived centuries ago and gave birth every year.
Did they wait for a little path without weeds?
— You know, Lilichka, — she said to her sister, — Tolstoy wrote that a woman's interest lies only in 'carnal love'…
— You've worn me out with your Tolstoy, — Lilichka retorted.
She took the scissors and fought with an excess cuticle.
— You'd be hard pressed to find a bigger jerk.
— And then there's that one, the wife of the American prosecutor — they said she 'wasn't interested in politics,' but she had sixteen children!
What politics, for heaven's sake?
— Oh, Lord, with one child I wasn't even interested in clothes, — Lilichka retorted.
She recalled how, when Marko was four months old, she ran out of all her trousers.
Her shape had gone somewhere, and all her trousers slipped off her hips.
And it wasn't that she had no time.
It was just that trousers weren't within her circle of interests back then.
She belted them and pretended that's how it was supposed to be.
But before New Year, when mannequins in beautiful skinny jeans stood in all the shop windows, she finally resolved and went to a shop.
There her golden boy got hot, started whining, the saleswoman took him in her arms, and Lilichka grabbed the first trousers a size too small and bought them.
They'd been her favorites ever since.
And dark shop windows on narrow streets and the cold wind between them still remind her of that feeling of helplessness, when you stand in trousers too big for you.
— Miyechka, I was a fool, I could have taken an interest in clothes.
I'll tell Marta about it, — Lilichka was still holding in her hands the phone with the photo of Marta's children that Maria had sent her.
They were just two sets of cheeks, because of which they couldn't open their eyes at all, which were besides narrow, like their father's.
Both boys had hair sticking up like a hedgehog's.
— Oh Lord, what on earth will they name them?
— said the grandmother.
— Let them name them so that I can remember, — she went on.
— Though what difference does it make?
Let them grow up healthy, and I'll die soon anyway.
Miyechka remembered that when grandfather died, she was alone at home with Lilya's Marko.
And she didn't know what to do.
They already knew, they were preparing, she saw that there was something on the windowsill suggesting it would happen soon, but how can you be ready for such a thing?
However much you prepare.
She understood that it had happened.
Then she came out of the room and saw Marko — he sat at the table, dark-haired, with little muscles clinging to his delicate little figure.
He sat as if nothing had happened, and absorbedly dipped a pancake into a little dish of honey.
The honey sometimes dripped onto his bare belly.
Then he carefully wiped it off with his little finger and licked it.
— Marko, — Miyechka said then, — grandfather is gone.
— Are there only bones left?
— Marko asked with interest.
— No, — said Miyechka.
— A person just stops breathing and the heart stops beating.
— Got it, — said Marko.
— Hug me, sweetheart.
Marko got up from the table, came up to Miyechka and hugged her for exactly three seconds.
That was exactly as much as his patience allowed, and exactly as long as he was prepared to part with the pancakes and honey.
And now, for the first time since that day, newborns would be brought into this house.
Not counting Lusya, who in a certain sense was also a newborn and was also on the threshold of changes too big for her, so small.
Along with the children, inspiration came to Miyechka, and she stopped suffering for a while, and at the same time found the equipment to uproot the orchard and plant a new one.
Apple Trees
— So what kind of children were born?
— asked Ustym, meeting Miyechka on the highway, as he had back when the roof was leaking.
— Chubby-cheeked, their little eyes haven't even opened yet, — she answered.
— Like kittens.
— Like kittens, — Miyechka repeated.
They uprooted the orchard loudly.
The trees crackled and fell, and it was painful for Miyechka to watch.
— Listen, — Ustym said to her.
— It's a law of nature, someone dies — someone is born.
If other people didn't die, these chubby-cheeked Japanese of yours couldn't have been born.
— That's true, — Miyechka sighed.
— But that doesn't make death any easier, — she had to shout to be heard over the roar of the machinery and the cracking of the trees.
The apple trees were immediately sawn into pieces.
This made it a little easier for Miyechka — they didn't lie helplessly, like the poplar in her childhood, but were immediately turned into something useful.
The day before, together with Lilichka, Greg and Nati, they'd gathered the apple harvest.
It was easy — either it was a poor harvest year, or these apple trees, lost in time and space, had simply forgotten how to give birth to apples.
There were a few crates of apples.

They — half-rotten, worm-eaten and crooked — stood in the middle of the yard, while the grandmother walked around them and said: 'It's for this, girls, that I boiled five hundred dumplings for all the workers, year after year.'
They tried to get rid of the apples as quickly as possible.
They baked them with nuts and honey and fed them to the children, made charlottes, pies and smoothies.
They grated them with carrot.
When the sun set, the grandmother went out to look at the crates, covered with her full skirt with little daisies, so the cats wouldn't sleep on the apples at night.
They did not grow fewer.
— Miyechka will uproot it and abandon it, — said the grandmother.
Maria shrugged.
After all, she had never managed to fully understand her children, and prophecies about them never once came true, even when professionals took up the matter.
— Or she'll plant it and abandon it, — the grandmother repeated.
Miyechka was thinking of planting plums — white and red ones.
Plums struck even greater horror into the grandmother than apples.
She recalled those thirty plum trees all over the yard and how she'd assisted her husband with the endless production of plum brandy, which not a single year came out right.
The cycle of plum brandy seemed an unceasing absurdity.
— The plum brandy, — Miyechka shouted over the cracking of the apple trees falling one after another, — Fumitaka will take on.
— Who?
— Ustym shouted.
— The Japanese man!

Miyechka kept assuring the grandmother that Fumitaka knew how to make plum brandy, that he'd plant an orchard here and take charge, and that he, Marta and the two boys would live here splendidly, the way she and grandfather had once been meant to live, but somehow it had never worked out.
— And how does he know about plum brandy?
— asked the grandmother.
— His father did this, — said Miyechka.
This was true.
Fumitaka's father made sweet plum wine.
